rules.statement: Code MUST NOT swallow exceptions with a bare 'except:' or 'except Exception: pass'. Name the exception you expect and handle it; a silent except hides the bug you most need to see.
rules.rationale: A bare except that silently passes is unambiguous bad practice in any Python codebase — it hides failures that would otherwise surface immediately. The fix is always the same: name the exception. This is the starter's blocking rule because it is trivially fixable and has zero false positives.

rules.statement: Public functions and classes MUST have a docstring that states their intent. A docstring is the contract a future reader — human or tool — relies on.
Importable library code MUST NOT use print(). Use a logger so output is structured, leveled, and routable. print() in shipped code is almost always a debugging leftover.

Source code MUST NOT contain hardcoded credentials — passwords, API keys, or secret tokens assigned directly to a string literal. Hardcoded secrets leak into version control. Move them to environment variables or a secrets manager.
rules.rationale: Hardcoded credentials are the most common cause of secret leaks in version-controlled repositories. Reporting (not blocking) on first adoption gives operators time to confirm exclusions before promoting to blocking.
